title: SEGMENT 3 — Gurdon's frogs: the nucleus has all the information

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — Gurdon's frogs: the nucleus has all the information
It is 1962.
A young British biologist named John Gurdon, working at Oxford, is about to do an experiment that nobody really believes will work.
He's going to test the idea that the nucleus of an adult cell — the DNA inside it — still contains all the information needed to make a whole organism.

If it does, you should be able to extract that nucleus, put it into an environment that knows how to use it, and watch a whole organism develop from a cell that used to be, say, a piece of frog intestine.
The technique is called somatic cell nuclear transfer, often abbreviated SCNT, and the procedure is exactly as audacious as it sounds.
Gurdon took a frog egg — a fertilized egg, or rather an unfertilized one ready to be fertilized — and he used ultraviolet light to destroy its own nucleus.
Now he had an enucleated egg, a cell with all the cytoplasm and machinery of an egg, but no DNA.
Then he took an adult frog intestinal epithelial cell — a fully differentiated cell from the gut lining of a tadpole — and he extracted its nucleus with a tiny micropipette.
He injected that adult nucleus into the enucleated egg.
Now think about what's happening biologically.
The egg cytoplasm is exquisitely tuned to start a new organism.
It's loaded with maternal RNAs, with transcription factors, with all the molecular machinery that normally interprets the fertilized nucleus and initiates development.
But the nucleus it just received isn't the nucleus of an egg.
It's the nucleus of an intestinal cell.
It's been carrying around the program "be a gut lining cell" for the past several weeks.
Its DNA has been packaged into a chromatin state that says "I am intestine.
" Its non-intestine genes are silenced.
The egg cytoplasm has no idea any of this.
The egg cytoplasm just sees a nucleus and starts trying to do what egg cytoplasm does — initiate embryonic development.
And here's the astonishing result.
In a small fraction of cases — Gurdon was very honest that his efficiency was low — the egg succeeded.
The intestinal nucleus, exposed to the egg cytoplasm's reprogramming factors, was forced to dismantle its intestinal program and adopt the embryonic program.
The egg started dividing.
It became an embryo.
It hatched into a tadpole.
The tadpole was a genetic clone of the original adult frog whose intestine had donated the nucleus.
Gurdon had created a frog from a gut cell.
Now stop for a second and think about what that experiment actually proved.
It proved two things, simultaneously, that were absolutely foundational for the rest of this story.
First, it proved that the DNA in an adult cell is not damaged or deleted during differentiation.
There was an alternative theory at the time — that during development, cells permanently lost the genes they didn't need anymore.
Maybe an intestinal cell had thrown away its neuron genes and its muscle genes, and that's why it couldn't become a neuron.
Gurdon's frog disproved that.
If the intestinal nucleus, taken alone, could produce an entire frog — with its neurons and muscles and skin and bones — then the intestinal nucleus must still contain all of those genes.
The information was still in there.
The DNA blueprint is preserved.
What differentiation does is restrict access to that blueprint, not erase parts of it.
Second, Gurdon's experiment proved that the cytoplasmic environment around the nucleus can reprogram the nucleus's state.
The egg cytoplasm was the reprogramming factor.

Whatever molecules the egg had — maternally deposited transcription factors, chromatin-remodeling enzymes, RNA, all of it — was apparently sufficient to wipe the intestinal program off the nucleus and impose the embryonic program.
The nucleus, by itself, would have stayed intestinal.
Put into the right environment, it became something else.
That insight — environment can reprogram nucleus — is the conceptual seed of Yamanaka's work, forty-four years later.
Because if the egg cytoplasm contains a set of factors that can reprogram an adult nucleus, the obvious next question is: what are those factors?
Can we identify them?
Can we, instead of using whole egg cytoplasm, just add the right purified molecules directly to a cell and reprogram it that way?
Yamanaka's screen — which we'll get to in Segment 5 — is, in essence, a search for those factors.
Forty-four years to go from "egg cytoplasm reprograms a nucleus" to "four specific transcription factors reprogram a nucleus.
Gurdon's work, by the way, took decades to be fully accepted.
His efficiency was low.
People assumed his successful clones must have come from contaminating embryonic cells in his preparations.

It took years of replication and refinement before the field grudgingly admitted that yes, adult nuclei could be reprogrammed by egg cytoplasm, and yes, this told us something deep about how cells work.
By the time he won the Nobel Prize alongside Yamanaka in 2012, his original frog experiment was fifty years old.
Science can be patient.
One more thing about Gurdon, just because it's such a nice story.
As a teenager in school, his biology teacher wrote on his report card that any ambition to do science was a "sheer waste of time" because Gurdon was so manifestly bad at the subject.
He kept the report card framed on the wall of his office for the rest of his career.
Pin that to the wall of your office too, if you ever feel discouraged.
